content:
I'd like to have a discussion about using (or not) Pin<_> in method signature, hopefully we can reach out to a conclusion and document it somewhere.

My understanding of Pin is largely based on the following conceptual model:

  • "pinned" is a non-reversible state of a memory object, once pinned, stay pinned until drop
  • Of course moving a pinned !Unpin object is UB, no matter the move itself is a safe API or not.

, and comes to Pin<>:

  • Having a Pin<_> proves the object is pinned.
  • Not having a Pin<_> (i.e. having a normal & or &mut or DerefMut) CANNOT prove the object is not pinned. The object may be pinned previously but the Pin<_> pointer is dropped.

Now if the above conceptual model is right, having a Pin<_> in the method signature means at least two things (for the sake of ease, let's say the object is !Unpin):

  1. Don't move inside:

    • the method won't move the object inside, since the method was only provides a Pin<_> there is no safe way to move the object. This is especially useful for trait (e.g. Future::polll()) or generic function.

  2. Don't move outside:

    • after the method called, the moving the object is UB, since Pin<_> proves the object was pinned when the method called. And moving something after pinned is UB.

The above is good enough to guide myself for whether use Pin<_> or not if I design something. Does it sound reasonable? (I do have some follow-ups, but in case I'm wrong, let me pause here for a while ;-))
Another point is that, once you've pinned something, you cannot safely get a &mut T to it, so any mutable method you want to be able to call after pinning must take self: Pin<&mut Self>

Alice Ryhl said:

Another point is that, once you've pinned something, you cannot safely get a &mut T to it, so any mutable method you want to be able to call after pinning must take self: Pin<&mut Self>

Thanks. That's related to something I want to discuss. Let's narrow this down to Pin<&mut T> and &mut T only. Based on what you said above, can I get the following conclusion?

  • For any !Unpin, if one of the methods takes Pin<&mut Self>, all other mutable methods should also take Pin<&mut Self>.

As Alice already said, having a &mut T means that it is not pinned (because I can freely mem::replace it). But for &T we do not know if it is pinned or not, since Pin<&T> implements Deref. With the same argument we know that the pointee is not pinned if we have a DerefMut.

Otherwise your understanding seems correct. I additionally want emphasize the drop guarantee that one has to uphold in addition to not moving. So for example if I treat some element of a vector as pinned and then call Vec::set_len (an unsafe function), then the pinned object will never be dropped, but it might be deallocated/the memory might be filled with another object. This is not allowed to happen and it would be UB.
I surely missed the different between &T and &mut T

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

Another point is that, once you've pinned something, you cannot safely get a &mut T to it, so any mutable method you want to be able to call after pinning must take self: Pin<&mut Self>

Thanks. That's related to something I want to discuss. Let's narrow this down to Pin<&mut T> and &mut T only. Based on what you said above, can I get the following conclusion?

  • For any !Unpin, if one of the methods takes Pin<&mut Self>, all other mutable methods should also take Pin<&mut Self>.

That depends on if you only want to use this type while it is pinned. If yes, then you can always use Pin<&mut Self>. But if you intend to

  1. create the value
  2. mutate it while not pinned and move it around some...
  3. at some point pin it
  4. and then mutate it again

then you will need normal &mut Self functions for 2 and Pin<&mut Self> functions for 4. Since the functions with Pin<&mut Self> might rely on the value staying pinned forever (so we cannot "just pin it for one call" and reuse the Pin<&mut Self> functions instead of declaring additional &mut Self funcs).

In userland rust code I have only really seen futures that use pinning and they are normally initialized and then immediately pinned, so I dont know if it makes sense to carry around some object that is modified and then pinned at some later point, but still needs modifications.

but what if I do

impl ... {
  pub fn foo(self: Pin<&mut Self>) -> Guard; // Guard doesn't have a constructor
  pub fn bar(&mut self, g: Guard); // bar requires not move, but use `Guard` as the proof
}

I'm not trying to be a langauge/UB lawyer, just want to get some rules for API signatures related to Pin we all agree in kernel.

you will not be able to call bar after calling foo on the same object.
(also the guard could be created with object A, but bar could be called on object B, which is not pinned)

y86 said:

you will not be able to call bar after calling foo on the same object.
(also the guard could be created with object A, but bar could be called on object B, which is not pinned)

Ah, good point on the other object, but why not able to call bar after calling foo? bar won't move the object.
how do you get a &mut Self when you only have a Pin<&mut Self>?
I was assuming that Self: !Unpin

You claim that bar won't move the object. But how do you know/guarantee that?

If the receiver is &mut self, it is allowed to move...

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

You claim that bar won't move the object. But how do you know/guarantee that?

If the receiver is &mut self, it is allowed to move...

The topic here is API design, i.e. the implementor knows
All down to a very simple question, when do I need a Pin<&mut Self> or Pin<&Self>.
The language will prevent you from calling bar because a Pin<&mut Self> provides no safe way to get a &mut T
Boqun, I think we need to go to the real issue here :)

Let's use a simple example as @y86 said above:

impl ... {
  pub fn foo(self: Pin<&mut Self);  // 4
  pub fn bar(&mut self);  // 2
}

Do we say foo(); bar(); is a UB regardless of whether bar moves the object or not?
you cannot call bar after calling foo since you cannot get a &mut Self after pinning the object.

y86 said:

you cannot call bar after calling foo since you cannot get a &mut Self after pinning the object.

I can get &mut Self unsafely, no?
yes that is possible
and that is not UB, but a footgun
I'm pretty aware that's a footgun, my question is more about should we leave it as a footgun, or should we make bar take a Pin<&mut Self>
what is the benefit of the footgun? I think it should be Pin<&mut Self> and if you need to call it both before and after pinning, then one needs to duplicate the function.

Not about benefit, just about "whose fault" ;-) Say I'm a user of foo and bar, if bar can be called after foo and bar takes &mut _. It looks to me it's the fault of the API design and makes user use the footgun.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Boqun, I think we need to go to the real issue here :)

I think I have plenty ;-) First, should lock() take a Pin<&Self>?

Boqun Feng said:

Not about benefit, just about "whose fault" ;-) Say I'm a user of foo and bar, if bar can be called after foo and bar takes &mut _. It looks to me it's the fault of the API design and makes user use the footgun.

not so sure if I understand correctly which scenario you are talking about, but as a user I would not use unsafe to call &mut Self after Pin<&mut Self>.

Boqun Feng said:

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Boqun, I think we need to go to the real issue here :)

I think I have plenty ;-) First, should lock() take a Pin<&Self>?

I would say that it would make sense to use Pin<&Self>, but with pin-init it will not be possible to create a e.g. mutex that is not pinned (so it would not be footgun to keep &self).
I would prefer to change it to Pin<&Self> since it also more clearly communicates this to the docs readers.
But unlocked spinlock is safe to move

It is &self at the moment due to the bad ergonomics of pin projection. Suppose we have a type that is something like:

struct X {
    some_field: u32,
    inner: Mutex<InnerX>,
}

Now suppose we have an x: &X. How do we access the inner instance of InnerX?

(By the safety requirements of the constructor of Mutex, we know that it must have been initialised, which means that the mutex _is_ pinned.)
(The comment above is about lock().)

Back to the topic I think Benno's idea sounds good, I add something for immutable references. Considering a pointer type of a method, here is some basic suggestion:

  • For mutable functions:
    • If it requires pinned or assumes pinned afterwards, takes a Pin<&mut >
    • If it doesn't requires pinned and may be called before pinned, takes a &mut self
    • If it turns out the above &mut self function may be called after pinned, introduce a different function, which takes a Pin<&mut>
  • For immutable functions
    • if it requires pinned or assumes pinned afterwards, takes a Pin<&>
    • otherwise make it take a &

Thoughts?

y86 said:

not so sure if I understand correctly which scenario you are talking about, but as a user I would not use unsafe to call &mut Self after Pin<&mut Self>.

This is why I brought up this topic. If a type only provides foo(Pin<&mut Self>)and bar(&mut self), then foo(); bar() is unusable for someone but usable for someone else. It's not a correctness issue (footgun code can be sound) but more a style issue. There is certainly the usage of Pin to get compiler happy vs the usage of Pin to make the most sense what Pin provides. Some clarification and stylish guide are helpful.

y86 said:

Boqun Feng said:

I think I have plenty ;-) First, should lock() take a Pin<&Self>?

I would say that it would make sense to use Pin<&Self>, but with pin-init it will not be possible to create a e.g. mutex that is not pinned (so it would not be footgun to keep &self).

Right, for Pin<&Self> vs &self, the only difference is "Don't move outside", in other word, Pin<_> only marks when the object pinned. So if a object is pinned since initialized, Pin<&Self> makes no difference to &self. Plus, we have the pin-projection issue Wedson mentioned.

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

not so sure if I understand correctly which scenario you are talking about, but as a user I would not use unsafe to call &mut Self after Pin<&mut Self>.

This is why I brought up this topic. If a type only provides foo(Pin<&mut Self>)and bar(&mut self), then foo(); bar() is unusable for someone but usable for someone else. It's not a correctness issue (footgun code can be sound) but more a style issue. There is certainly the usage of Pin to get compiler happy vs the usage of Pin to make the most sense what Pin provides. Some clarification and stylish guide are helpful.

I would expect as an API designer for users to not call foo(); bar(), as you need unsafe for that. Even if bar at the moment does not rely upon the fact that self is not pinned, I am allowed to change that.

This somewhat reminds me of the "What is unsafe?" discussion from a few months ago. When doing a lot of pin projections it is more readable to call Pin::get_unchecked_mut() once and then access the fields that one needs, rather than calling Pin::map_unchecked_mut() multiple times. But this results in a local variable which is &mut T, but it must not be moved otherwise it is UB.
I think in that discussion we reached the consensus that as long as this is explained by the safety comment and as long as this variable is only local to the function, we are ok with that.
This seems similar, but this time bar's signature communicates globally that it is allowed to move the value. If we decide to say in the docs of bar "this function does not move the given value, it always treats it as if it were pinned", then people could rely on that and call foo(); bar(), but I would be against this. Just create a new bar_pinned(self: Pin<&mut Self>), it is safer (users do not need unsafe to call it), it is clear that this is called while pinned. Additionally it allows us in the future, if we for some reason find that in bar() we could optimize something by doing some modification that is disallowed for Pin<&mut Self>, to change bar such that it does this modification, but leave bar_pinned as is.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

It is &self at the moment due to the bad ergonomics of pin projection. Suppose we have a type that is something like:

struct X {
    some_field: u32,
    inner: Mutex<InnerX>,
}

Now suppose we have an x: &X. How do we access the inner instance of InnerX?

(By the safety requirements of the constructor of Mutex, we know that it must have been initialised, which means that the mutex _is_ pinned.)

because it has a mutex, we should have access to a Pin<&X> and with (the missing field projection) we can get Pin<&Mutex<InnerX>>.
But this is unergonomic at the moment...

Advantages of using Pin<&>:

  • clearer from a documenting perspective and users are reminded that this object is pinned
  • when we later have pinnable things that exist also while unpinned, we already have the discipline to always use Pin<&> when we are talking about the pinned state

Disadvantages:

  • more to write, every pinned object now needs self: Pin<&Self> instead of &self which is very annoying (maybe macro to the rescue, or try to get something like &pin self to work?). I feel like this could alienate some of the C folks.
  • lack of pin projections makes this unergonomic, if we had pin-projections it would work nicely (example from above: x->inner.lock() where x: Pin<&X>)
  • users who see Pin<&> everywhere might be confused/have to learn what pinning is (but this seems inherent to me, so we cannot really avoid this by hiding the complexity behind &, as they might have to use Pin<&mut > anyways)

I am a bit torn on this one.
Yeah, lack of pin projection is the most annoying thing
I think I said this in some meeting, I will have a design meeting in late march about the projection rfc, so I hope that we can eventually have it :)

If a locked spinlock is also safe to move when there are no threads waiting for it, then it probably doesn't have to be pinned at all. See <https://github.com/MicrosoftDocs/sdk-api/pull/447> for an illustration where Windows docs were updated to give sufficient guarantees that the std mutex doesn't have to be heap allocated (i.e. can be moved) on windows.
A locked spinlock can't be moved due to borrowck anyway.

(maybe macro to the rescue, or try to get something like &pin self to work?

I guess you mean something for entire impls? (since e.g. an attribute macro for each method would even take longer to type).

&pin self sounds quite natural to me.

I feel like this could alienate some of the C folks.

It could go both ways! Some C folks may indeed think it is too much to learn, but others may actually like the clarity/explicitness, especially while learning.

bjorn3 said:

A locked spinlock can't be moved due to borrowck anyway.

It's possible to forget the guard and then move or forget the mutex in a locked state, but not if anyone is waiting for the lock.

Alice Ryhl said:

bjorn3 said:

A locked spinlock can't be moved due to borrowck anyway.

It's possible to forget the guard and then move or forget the mutex in a locked state, but not if anyone is waiting for the lock.

If lockdep is enabled, during lock(), lockdep will add the pointer to the lock into a held locks stack, and at unlock() time, lockdep will use the pointer to the lock and the stack to check whether the lock is held.

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

not so sure if I understand correctly which scenario you are talking about, but as a user I would not use unsafe to call &mut Self after Pin<&mut Self>.

This is why I brought up this topic. If a type only provides foo(Pin<&mut Self>)and bar(&mut self), then foo(); bar() is unusable for someone but usable for someone else. It's not a correctness issue (footgun code can be sound) but more a style issue. There is certainly the usage of Pin to get compiler happy vs the usage of Pin to make the most sense what Pin provides. Some clarification and stylish guide are helpful.

It's only an issue if some type is usable both if it's pinned or not, and the type is not Unpin? What type do you have in mind that satisfy this property?

Gary Guo said:

It's only an issue if some type is usable both if it's pinned or not, and the type is not Unpin? What type do you have in mind that satisfy this property?

First, I want to say it seems me there is no way to prove such a type doesn't exist.
You can craft such a type and I'll just call that API poorly designed. There's no need to prove non-existence of such a type for soundness.

Yeah, so maybe consider the following case:

First there exists a type TypeA : Unpin, has a few &mut self functions and other functions. And it has a few users.
And one day, someone wants to improve TypeA to work asynchronously by adding a work_struct in it. And that point TypeA becomes !Unpin. Note that a Work<TypeA> solution may not always work, since in the future TypeA may need a second async mechanism, e.g. timer_list.

If that happens, what would be the recommendation for people working on TypeA?
If you have a !Unpin type, it's highly unlikely that you want to have a method taking &mut self -- so you probably want to change signature to Pin<&mut Self>

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

bjorn3 said:

A locked spinlock can't be moved due to borrowck anyway.

It's possible to forget the guard and then move or forget the mutex in a locked state, but not if anyone is waiting for the lock.

If lockdep is enabled, during lock(), lockdep will add the pointer to the lock into a held locks stack, and at unlock() time, lockdep will use the pointer to the lock and the stack to check whether the lock is held.

Right. At least you can't unlock it anymore once you forget the mutex guard. No idea if that is enough for preventing UB though.
You can still attempt to lock it again, so if lockdep also accesses the pointer to the stack that is an issue.

Boqun Feng said:

Yeah, so maybe consider the following case:

First there exists a type TypeA : Unpin, has a few &mut self functions and other functions. And it has a few users.
And one day, someone wants to improve TypeA to work asynchronously by adding a work_struct in it. And that point TypeA becomes !Unpin. Note that a Work<TypeA> solution may not always work, since in the future TypeA may need a second async mechanism, e.g. timer_list.

If that happens, what would be the recommendation for people working on TypeA?

To me such a change would be a breaking change. Since it could break existing code. When you really need to do such a change, I would suggest to also change the signatures to Pin<&mut Self> and only allow pinned initialization. Since there seems to be no harm to just leave it pinned forever.

bjorn3 said:

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

bjorn3 said:

A locked spinlock can't be moved due to borrowck anyway.

It's possible to forget the guard and then move or forget the mutex in a locked state, but not if anyone is waiting for the lock.

If lockdep is enabled, during lock(), lockdep will add the pointer to the lock into a held locks stack, and at unlock() time, lockdep will use the pointer to the lock and the stack to check whether the lock is held.

Right. At least you can't unlock it anymore once you forget the mutex guard. No idea if that is enough for preventing UB though.

Well, you can unlock unsafely, unlock() safety requirement is that "you must be the owner" and nothing about pinned.

the only reason for having both &mut self and Pin<&mut Self> methods at the same time is the need for some accumulation prior to pinning, but this could be factored out to happen entirely before pinned initialization and then you only need Pin<&mut Self> methods.

y86 said:

the only reason for having both &mut self and Pin<&mut Self> methods at the same time is the need for some accumulation prior to pinning, but this could be factored out to happen entirely before pinned initialization and then you only need Pin<&mut Self> methods.

I agree, and I also agree if we go ahead and say every !Unpin type needs to be pinned initialized before use if it looks reasonable to every one.

Hmm.. now think about it, replacing all &mut selfs with self: Pin<&mut self>s doesn't seems a bad idea? For Unpin, you still have as_mut(), and for !Unpin, that's what we want, plus there is no need to change the method signature when the types go Unpin -> !Unpin or vice versa

Boqun Feng said:

Hmm.. now think about it, replacing all &mut selfs with self: Pin<&mut self>s doesn't seems a bad idea? For Unpin, you still have as_mut(), and for !Unpin, that's what we want, plus there is no need to change the method signature when the types go Unpin -> !Unpin or vice versa

Of course, the pre-condition is all !Unpin should be pinned initialized.

Boqun Feng said:

Hmm.. now think about it, replacing all &mut selfs with self: Pin<&mut self>s doesn't seems a bad idea? For Unpin, you still have as_mut(), and for !Unpin, that's what we want, plus there is no need to change the method signature when the types go Unpin -> !Unpin or vice versa

you dont even need as_mut(), because Pin<&mut Unpin> implements DerefMut. But reborrowing becomes more of a hassle.

y86 said:

Boqun Feng said:

Hmm.. now think about it, replacing all &mut selfs with self: Pin<&mut self>s doesn't seems a bad idea? For Unpin, you still have as_mut(), and for !Unpin, that's what we want, plus there is no need to change the method signature when the types go Unpin -> !Unpin or vice versa

you dont even need as_mut(), because Pin<&mut Unpin> implements DerefMut. But reborrowing becomes more of a hassle.

Reborrowing becomes pin projection? ;-)
not sure how that would help, I think it would actually be as_mut() instead of doing &mut *

y86 said:

not sure how that would help, I think it would actually be as_mut() instead of doing &mut *

What's the reborrow scene in your mind?

with &mut:

struct Foo;

fn bar(_foo: &mut Foo) {}

fn main() {
    let foo = &mut Foo;
    bar(foo);
    bar(foo);
    bar(foo);
    bar(foo);
    bar(foo);
}

with Pin<&mut>:

use std::pin::Pin;
struct Foo;

fn bar(_foo: Pin<&mut Foo>) {}

fn main() {
    let foo = &mut Foo;
    let mut foo = Pin::new(foo);
    bar(foo.as_mut());
    bar(foo.as_mut());
    bar(foo.as_mut());
    bar(foo.as_mut());
    bar(foo.as_mut());
}
27 messages were moved here from #General > Pin<_> in method signature by Boqun Feng.

@_Notification Bot|100006 said:

27 messages were moved here from #General > Pin<_> in method signature by Boqun Feng.

(sorry, I was trying to move just one message from the discussion)

Below is the current version I'm considering to add as some sort of guidelines for using Pin. I probably open a Github issue first for boarder discussion before I send a patch. It's still need some polishing, but I want to see whether we can get on the same page.

When do you need the function to take Pin<_> as the argument? If any of the following is true:

  • The function expects the object pinned (i.e. the object should have a fixed address), this is a sign of starting relying on pin safety guarantee.
    • There are rare cases that the pin safety guarantee (once pinned, always pinned) can be broken by users, if these are allowed, see UNPIN comments below
  • The function is a mutable one (i.e. it would take a &mut self) and may be called after the object is pinned. Reason: otherwise users may need to use unsafe to call these function.
    • What about the function may be called before pinned? A duplicate &mut self version or rely on pin-init so all mutable functions take Pin<&mut Self> ???

UNPIN comments:

  • Put an # Unpin comments on the functions that make the object movable again (e.g. removing the object from a global list), notes these functions themselves have to be a Pin<_> function. For example,
struct A {
    ...
    _pin: PhantomPinned,
}

impl A {
    /// Puts on a list for execution.
    pub fn queue(self: Pin<&mut Self>)
    /// # Unpin
    ///
    /// Removes the object from the global list when return,
    /// can be moved after this.
    pub fn wait_for_finish(self: Pin<&mut Self>)
}

Then if these # Unpin functions are used to make the objects movable. Add a UNSOUND: UNPIN: comment as the move point, e.g.

let a = A::new(..);

// UNSOUND: SAFETY: `a` may be moved afterwards, but that's after
// a Unpin function.
let pin = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&mut a)};

pin.as_mut().queue();

// UNSOUND: UNPIN: `wait_for_finish()` makes `a` movable again.
pin.wait_for_finish();

// UNSOUND: Moves `a`.
let b = Box::new(a);
What you describe is not unsound. Soundness is a property of API, not code.
If you want to unpin something that's pinned and !Unpin, a better mental model would be to treat it as dropped and reconstructed in-place.

Gary Guo said:

What you describe is not unsound. Soundness is a property of API, not code.

I used unsound because it's unsound regarding Pin API, no?
Depends.
If you control a type, you control its destructor -- therefore it's perfectly valid to say that the destructor just removes references from global list.
It's a move after pinned for !Unpin.

Boqun Feng said:

It's a move after pinned for !Unpin.

I think that's unsound to Pin API, correct?

Gary Guo said:

If you want to unpin something that's pinned and !Unpin, a better mental model would be to treat it as dropped and reconstructed in-place.

^
You can model your wait_for_finish as doing a drop_in_place followed by constructing a new A
and then it's not violating Pin's contract.
Ah, I see what you mean, let me think about it.
https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/pin/index.html#drop-guarantee
Concretely, for pinned data you have to maintain the invariant that its memory will not get invalidated or repurposed from the moment it gets pinned until when drop is called.
Is drop_in_place + rescontructing considered as invalidated or repurposed?
drop_in_place is calling the drop.
Make sense. Look like we can move a pinned !Unpin soundly?

Gary Guo said:

You can model your wait_for_finish as doing a drop_in_place followed by constructing a new A

Here come the tricky part: what if the drop function of A has some side-effect (say, increasing some counters for statistics purpose), by ”modeling", do I need to do the same in wait_for_finsh?
If it's safe to not increase it, then you don't.
If doing so is required to remove global reference, then of course you would have to do that, but not otherwise.
Ok, so basically, if a function behaves like some ops;+ drop_in_place()+write(), it's an # Unpin function. Works for me ;-)
